title: Captaincy of Rio de Janeiro

infobox.value: [São Sebastião do Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro")

was established in the northern portion of the [Captaincy of São Vicente](/wiki/Captaincy_of_S%C3%A3o_Vicente "Captaincy of São Vicente"), encompassing territory from [Macaé](/wiki/Maca%C3%A9 "Macaé") (now part of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)")) to [Caraguatatuba](/wiki/Caraguatatuba "Caraguatatuba") (now part of [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)")). This region had been abandoned by its *[donatário](/wiki/Donat%C3%A1rio "Donatário")* [Martim Afonso de Sousa](/wiki/Martim_Afonso_de_Sousa "Martim Afonso de Sousa"), who, uninterested in its settlement, directed his attention and resources to the area along the current [São Paulo coast](/w/index.php?title=Coast_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Coast of São Paulo (page does not exist)").[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

Former territorial division of Brazil

The **Captaincy of Rio de Janeiro** was established in the northern portion of the [Captaincy of São Vicente](/wiki/Captaincy_of_S%C3%A3o_Vicente "Captaincy of São Vicente"), encompassing territory from [Macaé](/wiki/Maca%C3%A9 "Macaé") (now part of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)")) to [Caraguatatuba](/wiki/Caraguatatuba "Caraguatatuba") (now part of [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)")). This region had been abandoned by its *[donatário](/wiki/Donat%C3%A1rio "Donatário")* [Martim Afonso de Sousa](/wiki/Martim_Afonso_de_Sousa "Martim Afonso de Sousa"), who, uninterested in its settlement, directed his attention and resources to the area along the current [São Paulo coast](/w/index.php?title=Coast_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Coast of São Paulo (page does not exist)").[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Captaincy_of_Rio_de_Janeiro&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 16th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Captaincy_of_Rio_de_Janeiro&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 16th century")]

Since it was not occupied by the Portuguese, the region of [Guanabara Bay](/wiki/Guanabara_Bay "Guanabara Bay") experienced an attempt at colonization by the [French](/wiki/France "France") between 1555 and 1567, known as [France Antarctique](/wiki/France_Antarctique "France Antarctique"). In response, [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") founded the city of [São Sebastião do Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") in 1565 and, in 1567, established the [Royal Captaincy](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Captaincy&action=edit&redlink=1 "Royal Captaincy (page does not exist)") of Rio de Janeiro, appointing [Estácio de Sá](/wiki/Est%C3%A1cio_de_S%C3%A1 "Estácio de Sá") as its first representative. He died later that same year.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) In 1570, the then governor of the Captaincy of Rio de Janeiro, [Antônio Salema](/w/index.php?title=Ant%C3%B4nio_Salema&action=edit&redlink=1 "Antônio Salema (page does not exist)"), assembled an army of Portuguese troops supported by catechized indigenous people to eliminate the French-Tamoio dominance that had persisted for twenty years along the coast. Fearing the loss of their territories, the Tamoios, who remained allied with the French, were largely defeated during the conflict known as the [Cabo Frio War](/w/index.php?title=Cabo_Frio_War&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cabo Frio War (page does not exist)"), which ended in 1575.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### 17th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Captaincy_of_Rio_de_Janeiro&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 17th century")]

In 1619, the [Captaincy of São Tomé](/wiki/Captaincy_of_S%C3%A3o_Tom%C3%A9 "Captaincy of São Tomé") was ceded by its *donatário* and returned to the [Portuguese Empire](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire"). A significant portion of its territory, extending from the area of the present-day city of Macaé to [Itapemirim](/wiki/Itapemirim,_Esp%C3%ADrito_Santo "Itapemirim, Espírito Santo") in [Espírito Santo](/wiki/Esp%C3%ADrito_Santo "Espírito Santo"), including the largely unexplored interior, was incorporated into the Captaincy of Rio de Janeiro. In 1674, the Captaincy of Paraíba do Sul, also known as the Captaincy of Campos dos Goytacazes, was established by subdividing the northern part of the Captaincy of Rio de Janeiro. This new captaincy covered a region similar to that previously occupied by the Captaincy of São Tomé.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 1679, after the [Restoration of Portugal's Independence](/wiki/Portuguese_Restoration_War "Portuguese Restoration War") from [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), Governor Manuel Lobo was ordered to establish a colony on the eastern side of the [River Plate](/wiki/R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "Río de la Plata"), which he named [Colonia del Santíssimo Sacramento](/wiki/Colonia_del_Sacramento "Colonia del Sacramento"), in an expedition that gathered 400 soldiers recruited in Rio de Janeiro and São Paulo.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Four ships were dispatched, loaded with supplies and armed with 18 cannons.[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7)

### 18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Captaincy_of_Rio_de_Janeiro&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 18th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Vu%C3%AB_en_perspective_De_Riougenaire%2C_Ville_de_la_Merrique.jpg/330px-Vu%C3%AB_en_perspective_De_Riougenaire%2C_Ville_de_la_Merrique.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vu%C3%AB_en_perspective_De_Riougenaire,_Ville_de_la_Merrique.jpg)

*Vuë en pevpective De Riougenaire Ville de la Merrique...*, drawing by François Moyen depicting the town of Rio de Janeiro in 1744

In 1720, the towns of [Angra dos Reis](/wiki/Angra_dos_Reis "Angra dos Reis") and [Paraty](/wiki/Paraty "Paraty") were transferred to the [Captaincy of São Paulo](/wiki/Captaincy_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Captaincy of São Paulo") and remained until 1727, when they were reincorporated to Rio de Janeiro.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In 1738, with the Portuguese expansion to the south, the [Captaincy of Santa Catarina](/wiki/Captaincy_of_Santa_Catarina "Captaincy of Santa Catarina") was founded in the territory of the Captaincy of São Paulo, which was subordinated to the Captaincy of Rio de Janeiro.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In 1748, Portugal decided that the Captaincy of São Paulo would be subordinated to the Captaincy of Rio de Janeiro, which was then tasked with administering lands up to the coastal region of present-day [Rio Grande do Sul](/wiki/Rio_Grande_do_Sul "Rio Grande do Sul").[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 1750, through the [Treaty of Madrid](/wiki/Treaty_of_Madrid_(13_January_1750) "Treaty of Madrid (13 January 1750)"), the lands of the [Misiones Orientales](/wiki/Misiones_Orientales "Misiones Orientales") and the [Pampas](/wiki/Pampas "Pampas") of Rio Grande do Sul were also incorporated into the administration of Rio de Janeiro, in exchange for the Colonia del Sacramento to the Spanish.[[11]](#cite_note-11) That same year, the lands of the now-extinct Captaincy of Paraíba do Sul were also incorporated, making the Captaincy of Rio de Janeiro reach its greatest historical extent. In 1753, the [Captaincy of Itanhaém](/wiki/Captaincy_of_Itanha%C3%A9m "Captaincy of Itanhaém") was incorporated into Rio de Janeiro, and the area previously belonging to the Captaincy of Paraíba do Sul was reassigned to the [Captaincy of Espírito Santo](/wiki/Captaincy_of_Esp%C3%ADrito_Santo "Captaincy of Espírito Santo"), which remained part of Rio de Janeiro until it was re-established as a separate captaincy in 1832.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

In 1760, the [Captaincy of Rio Grande de São Pedro](/w/index.php?title=Captaincy_of_Rio_Grande_de_S%C3%A3o_Pedro&action=edit&redlink=1 "Captaincy of Rio Grande de São Pedro (page does not exist)") was established, remaining subordinate to the Captaincy of Rio de Janeiro. The following year, after the [Guaraní War](/wiki/Guaran%C3%AD_War "Guaraní War"), the captaincy lost the territory of the Misiones Orientales upon signing the [Treaty of El Pardo](/wiki/Treaty_of_El_Pardo_(1761) "Treaty of El Pardo (1761)") with the Spanish but gained back the Colonia del Sacramento.[[13]](#cite_note-:3-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In 1763, the Portuguese Empire decided to transfer the capital of the [State of Brazil](/wiki/State_of_Brazil "State of Brazil") from [Salvador](/wiki/Salvador,_Bahia "Salvador, Bahia") to the city of São Sebastião do Rio de Janeiro. This relocation aimed to improve control over the production in the [Captaincy of Minas Gerais](/w/index.php?title=Captaincy_of_Minas_Gerais&action=edit&redlink=1 "Captaincy of Minas Gerais (page does not exist)"), whose goods were originally transported through the port of Paraty (*Caminho Velho*) but were later redirected to the port of Rio de Janeiro (*[Caminho Novo](/wiki/Estrada_Real "Estrada Real")*).[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) Two years later, São Paulo regained its autonomy under the administration of [Luís António de Sousa Botelho Mourão](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Ant%C3%B3nio_de_Sousa_Botelho_Mour%C3%A3o "Luís António de Sousa Botelho Mourão"), the third [Morgado de Mateus](/w/index.php?title=Morgado_de_Mateus&action=edit&redlink=1 "Morgado de Mateus (page does not exist)"), who created several towns in São Paulo territory.[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7)

In 1777, Spanish forces occupied the Captaincy of Santa Catarina but returned it the following year after the [Treaty of San Ildefonso](/wiki/First_Treaty_of_San_Ildefonso "First Treaty of San Ildefonso"), which granted them the Pampas region of Rio Grande do Sul and the Colonia del Sacramento.[[17]](#cite_note-17) At the end of the year, another [treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_El_Pardo_(1778) "Treaty of El Pardo (1778)") will determine the return of the Pampas to the Captaincy of Rio Grande de São Pedro.[[13]](#cite_note-:3-13)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Captaincy_of_Rio_de_Janeiro&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 19th century")]

In 1804, still subordinated to Rio de Janeiro, the Captaincy of Rio Grande de São Pedro annexed the lands of the Misiones and extended to the [Uruguay River](/wiki/Uruguay_River "Uruguay River"). Three years later, it gained autonomy from Rio de Janeiro, taking control of the Captaincy of Santa Catarina and getting the name of Captaincy of São Pedro do Rio Grande do Sul.[[13]](#cite_note-:3-13)

On February 28, 1821, by the decision of the Portuguese Empire, the captaincies of the [Kingdom of Brazil](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Brazil "Kingdom of Brazil") became [provinces](/wiki/Province "Province"); the [Province of Rio de Janeiro](/w/index.php?title=Province_of_Rio_de_Janeiro&action=edit&redlink=1 "Province of Rio de Janeiro (page does not exist)") was established, initially remaining under direct administration by the crown.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

During the [colonial period](/wiki/Colonial_Brazil "Colonial Brazil"), especially when Rio de Janeiro was a captaincy, several towns were founded, in addition to the city of São Sebastião do Rio de Janeiro, which served as its administrative center: [Angra dos Santos Reis da Ilha Grande](/wiki/Angra_dos_Reis "Angra dos Reis") (1608), [Santa Helena](/wiki/Cabo_Frio "Cabo Frio") (later Nossa Senhora da Assunção de Cabo Frio, 1615), [Nossa Senhora dos Remédios de Paraty](/wiki/Paraty "Paraty") (1667), [São Salvador dos Campos](/wiki/Campos_dos_Goytacazes "Campos dos Goytacazes") (1677), [Santo Antônio de Sá](/wiki/Cachoeiras_de_Macacu "Cachoeiras de Macacu") (1679), [Nossa Senhora da Piedade de Magé](/wiki/Mag%C3%A9 "Magé") (1696), [São João do Paraíba](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Jo%C3%A3o_da_Barra "São João da Barra") (1676), [Vila Nova de São José del Rei](/wiki/Itabora%C3%AD "Itaboraí"), [São João Marcos](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Jo%C3%A3o_Marcos "São João Marcos") (1733), [Nossa Senhora da Conceição da Paraíba Nova](/wiki/Resende,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Resende, Rio de Janeiro") (1756) and [Itaguaí](/wiki/Itagua%C3%AD "Itaguaí") (1818).[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

## See also
